On retry, DDx synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt whose <diff> section is the output of `git show <resultRev>`. When the prior attempt's landing sequence appended an evidence commit of the form `chore: add execution evidence [<attempt_id>]` that added `.ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/embedded/agent-*.jsonl` (full per-event NDJSON session log) as a tracked file, `resultRev` is that evidence commit's SHA (because `landEvidence` sets `result.NewTip = sha` and the orchestrator copies `land.NewTip` into `res.ResultRev`). `git show` of the evidence commit emits the entire session log diff with a `+` prefix per line. Session logs are 5-50k lines; the retry prompt exceeds every public context window.

## Category / severity / surface

- Category: correctness / resource-exhaustion
- Severity: high
- Surface: `ddx agent execute-bead` retry path triggered by `kind:review-malfunction` (and any other flow that re-synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt from a git diff/show since base_rev).

## Full bug description (verbatim)

On retry, DDx synthesizes a <bead-review> prompt whose <diff> section is the raw `git diff <base_rev>..<result_rev>` of the prior attempt. The prior attempt's landing sequence includes an evidence commit of the form:

    chore: add execution evidence [<attempt_id>]

that adds `.ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/embedded/agent-*.jsonl` — the full per-event NDJSON session log of the prior attempt — as a tracked file. Because that file is *added* in the diff range, the diff includes it in full with a `+` prefix on every line. Session logs are routinely 5-50k lines. One reasonable fix iteration is enough to push the retry prompt past 1-2 million tokens, which no currently supported model can accept — the provider streams back an n_keep > n_ctx error and the retry crashes without a request round-trip.

This is a self-poisoning loop: the "evidence" DDx commits to make attempts auditable is the exact artifact that makes the next attempt undeliverable.

## Reproduction (observed live 2026-04-17 in nexiq)

1. Bead `hx-dd4ab35f` claimed by execute-loop worker (harness=agent, model=qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b, provider=vidar).
2. Attempt `20260417T211600-d6a7dee6` succeeded and landed two commits: `cb5e733` (implementation + regression test [hx-dd4ab35f]) and `9a006dd` (chore: add execution evidence [20260417T211600-], adds `.ddx/executions/20260417T211600-d6a7dee6/embedded/agent-1776460560227794836.jsonl`, +20,623 lines).
3. Post-merge review emitted `BLOCK` without a rationale; DDx recorded `kind:review-malfunction` and reopened the bead for retry.
4. Retry `20260417T212928-27f9054a` synthesized `.ddx/executions/20260417T212928-27f9054a/prompt.md` of 6,307,997 bytes; `<bead-review>` block = 6,307,969 bytes; 99.9998% is the diff of the prior evidence commit, 99%+ of that diff is the session log file. Rest of <bead-review> (title/description/acceptance/labels/governing) is ~28 bytes.
5. lmstudio responded: "n_keep: 2,274,074 >= n_ctx: 196,608". Worker recorded failed attempt with zero usage. Same pattern on vidar (n_keep: 1,169,147) and bragi (n_keep: 2,274,074), all running qwen3.6-35b-a3b at max_context_length 262144. Provider/model is irrelevant — prompt exceeds every public context window.

## DDx code-path verification (Claude investigation 2026-04-17)

Relevant files and line ranges in /Users/erik/Projects/ddx:

- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:553-561` — `DefaultBeadReviewer.gitShow(rev)` runs `git -C <projectRoot> show <rev>` with no pathspec. Output is injected raw into `<diff>` by `BuildReviewPrompt` at `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:383-384`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_review.go:427-431` — reviewer fetches `diff := r.gitShow(resultRev)` then builds the prompt.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go:540-555` (`landEvidence`) — creates `chore: add execution evidence [<short>]` and sets `result.NewTip = sha`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go:428-434` — `RealLandingGitOps.StageDir` runs `git add -- <relPath>` on the entire `.ddx/executions/<attempt>/` directory with no exclusions for `embedded/`.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_orchestrator.go:250-251` — after Land, `res.ResultRev = land.NewTip`, so `ResultRev` ends up pointing at the evidence commit. The reviewer then runs `git show <evidence-commit>`, which is literally the dump of the session log.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:1135-1164` (`VerifyCleanWorktree`) — also stages the evidence dir via `git add -- <evidenceDir>` and commits `chore: add execution evidence [<short>]`; same no-exclusion pattern.
- `cli/cmd/bead_review.go:162-171` (`beadReviewGitShow`) — mirror of the same bug in the standalone `ddx bead review` command.

Confirmation the bug reporter's claim about `:(exclude).ddx/executions/*/embedded`:
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:269-283` (`SynthesizeCommit`) — the exclusion pathspec IS present here; the bug is that the evidence-landing commit path (`StageDir` + `VerifyCleanWorktree`) and the review path (`git show <rev>`) do not share this exclusion.
- `cli/cmd/init.go:19-46` — the gitignore rules DO include `.ddx/executions/*/embedded/`, but `git add -- <dir>` on pre-existing or explicitly-added paths can still stage, and older workspaces may pre-date this gitignore (matches nexiq's scenario where the evidence commit landed the session log).

## Deviation from bug description (minor)

The bug text describes the diff as `git diff <base_rev>..<result_rev>`. The code actually does `git show <resultRev>` (single-rev show). The self-poisoning mechanism is functionally identical: when `resultRev` points at the evidence commit (`landEvidence` + `res.ResultRev = land.NewTip`), `git show` emits the entire `+`-prefixed session log. All suggested fixes still apply.

## Suggested fixes (ascending scope)

1. Exclude execution-evidence paths from bead-review diffs by passing a pathspec to the `git show`/`git diff` call in the prompt builder: `-- . ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/embedded/*' ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/prompt.md' ':(exclude).ddx/executions/*/usage.json'`. The same exclusion already exists at `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go:269-283` for `SynthesizeCommit`; replicate it in `DefaultBeadReviewer.gitShow` (`execute_bead_review.go:553-561`), `beadReviewGitShow` (`cli/cmd/bead_review.go:162-171`), `landEvidence` → `StageDir` (`execute_bead_land.go:428-434,540-555`), and `VerifyCleanWorktree` (`execute_bead.go:1135-1164`).
2. Substitute, don't reproduce. When a tracked-file addition exceeds a byte/line budget in the diff, replace it with a one-line stub such as: `+++ b/.ddx/executions/.../agent-*.jsonl  (+20,623 lines of session evidence — omitted from review prompt)`.
3. Stop committing raw session logs as tracked evidence. Move `.ddx/executions/*/embedded/*.jsonl` to a gitignored store, or a separate `refs/ddx/evidence/*` ref analogous to `refs/ddx/iterations/*`.
4. Cap the <bead-review> prompt size with a hard budget (e.g., 150k tokens). Elide to summary + log warning when exceeded.

(1) is the minimal surgical fix; (3) is the correct long-term fix.

- Bloated retry prompt: `.ddx/executions/20260417T212928-27f9054a/prompt.md` (6,307,997 bytes; <bead-review> body = 6,307,969 bytes).
- Successful prior attempt: `.ddx/executions/20260417T211600-d6a7dee6/` (landed cb5e733 + 9a006dd).
- Failing agent session: `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-1776460487823541600.jsonl` (session.end payload has the n_keep/n_ctx error verbatim).

Delete `agent.routing.profile_ladders`, `agent.routing.model_overrides`, the `--escalate` and `--override-model` flags, all their consumers, and the deprecation warnings. The duplicated routing layer is dead — the corpse should not haunt every config load.

The migration `ddx-87fb72c2` made these opt-in. This bead completes the migration: opt-in becomes opt-out becomes gone.

## Scope (delete with prejudice)

### Config schema
- `cli/internal/config/types.go` — delete `RoutingConfig.ProfileLadders` and `RoutingConfig.ModelOverrides` fields (and `defaultProfileLadders`, `DefaultProfileLadders()`).
- `cli/internal/config/schema/config.schema.json` — delete the `profile_ladders` and `model_overrides` properties.
- `cli/internal/config/config.go` — delete the deprecation warning code paths that detect these fields and print the migration hint. Hard reject any project config that still sets them: clear error pointing at the (then-archived) migration doc, exit non-zero. Same blast radius as `default_harness` already gets.
- `cli/internal/config/routing_migration_test.go` — delete or repurpose to test the new hard-reject behavior.

### CLI flags
- `cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go` — delete `--escalate` and `--override-model` flag definitions and their plumbing into `WorkerSpec` / RouteRequest. The `WorkerSpec.Escalate` field added by `ddx-c7081f89` becomes dead and goes too.
- `cli/cmd/agent_execute_loop_*.go` — same.
- Any flag wiring in the worker submit path (`cli/internal/server/server.go`, `cli/internal/server/workers.go`).

### Helpers and consumers
- `cli/internal/agent/profile_ladder.go` — delete entirely (`ResolveProfileLadder`, `ResolveTierModelRef`, `NormalizeRoutingProfile`'s ladder semantics, the test seam call counters, `DefaultRoutingProfile`).
- `cli/internal/escalation/escalation.go` — delete `AdaptiveMinTier`, `AdaptiveMinTierResult`, `AdaptiveMinTierThreshold`, `AdaptiveMinTierMinSamples`, `TierResolver`, `classifyAttemptTier`, `TierOrder`, `TiersInRange`, `tierIndex`. Keep `EscalationSummary`, `TierAttemptRecord`, `BuildEscalationSummary`, `AppendEscalationSummaryEvent`, `EscalationWinningExhausted`, `EscalatableStatuses`, `ShouldEscalate`, `IsInfrastructureFailure`, `ProviderCooldownDuration`, `FormatTierAttemptBody`, `BeadEventAppender`, `SuccessStatus` — these are still used by other paths (loop status, bead events, cooldown).
- `cli/internal/server/workers.go:658-820` — delete the entire escalation-on branch (the `if !escalationEnabled { ... } else { ...tier loop... }` split). `escalationEnabled` always false; collapse to the single-call path.
- `cli/cmd/agent_route_status.go` — drop the `--adaptive` flag and `reset` subcommand if they target the now-deleted state. Keep the rest of route-status.
- `docs/adaptive-min-tier.md` — delete.

### Documentation
- `docs/migrations/routing-config.md` — delete (or replace with a one-line stub: "agent.routing.profile_ladders and agent.routing.model_overrides were removed in vX.Y.Z; configs that still set them are rejected at load.").
- `cli/internal/server/frontend/README.md`, `CHANGELOG.md` — search and remove any user-facing references to `--escalate` or `--override-model`.

### Tests
- Delete tests that exclusively cover the deleted paths: `*_escalation_*_test.go`, `profile_ladder_test.go`, `routing_migration_test.go`, etc. Where a test covers BOTH a deleted path and a kept path, narrow it to the kept path.
- Add one test that asserts `ddx work --once --local` on a project that still has `routing.profile_ladders` in its config exits non-zero with an error message naming the field.

## Affirmative non-goals

- Do **not** preserve any "transition" behavior. There is no warning-then-error grace period; `ddx-87fb72c2` already shipped the warning. This bead is the deletion.
- Do **not** keep dead `Escalate` / `MinTier` / `MaxTier` / `ModelRef` fields on `WorkerSpec` "for future use". If something needs them later, file a fresh bead.
- Do **not** keep the helpers in case some out-of-tree consumer imports them. DDx is a binary, not a library.
- Do **not** touch agent SDK internals — `ProfileEscalationLadder` and `escalateProfileLadder` live agent-side and stay.

## Out of scope

- Default profile change (separate bead if pursued).
- HistoricalSuccess injection into `RouteRequest.Inputs` (separate bead).
- Bead-id resolver bug (`ddx-7eab13a6`) — already filed.
- The 12 malformed-ID beads — `ddx-7eab13a6` covers their repair.

## Acceptance

## Rollback

That's useless for triage — the operator cannot tell whether:\n- the bead is genuinely already satisfied (work present in a prior commit)\n- the agent gave up because it couldn't find the relevant files\n- the agent classified the task as ambiguous or too risky\n- the agent hit a tool limit or context cap\n- the prompt contract was unclear\n\nThe fix is to make no_changes a structured result that carries a short rationale from the agent itself, written at the point where the agent decided not to commit. The bulk of this unification **already shipped** as `ddx-87fb72c2` (epic `ddx-fdd3ea36`) before this epic was filed:

- `agent.routing.profile_ladders` and `agent.routing.model_overrides` are now opt-in (consulted only with `--escalate` / `--override-model`).
- The default execute path on the CLI (`cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go`) calls `ResolveRoute` once per attempt instead of iterating a tier ladder.
- `agent.routing.default_harness` was removed (hard error if still present).
- `agent.ResolveTierCandidates` (from `ddx-5538aa5b`) replaced the broken literal-tier-name-as-Model pattern; tier resolution now consults the catalog.
- See `docs/migrations/routing-config.md` for the migration the user-facing CLI surface follows.

**Remaining gap (this epic now scopes only this):** the server worker path in `cli/internal/server/workers.go:650` was not updated by `ddx-87fb72c2`. It still derives `escalationEnabled := spec.Harness == "" && spec.Model == ""` instead of mirroring the CLI's `escalate && harness == "" && model == ""`. That mismatch is why `ddx work` (default mode, server-submit) still hits "all tiers exhausted — no viable provider" with `model_overrides` pointing at non-served models, while `ddx work --local --no-adaptive-min-tier` succeeds. Verified live in this session: `worker-20260429T033325-bedf` failed; `--no-adaptive-min-tier` got `ddx-98e6e9ef` to merge at `115b47c0` on local-tier qwen for zero cost.

Sole child: `ddx-c7081f89`. Once that lands, this epic closes.

## Strategic direction (preserved for future reference)

DDx embeds the agent SDK as a Go library to produce traceable execution units: `(bead, input_state) → (output_state, processing_metadata)`. DDx owns the execution unit and its metadata (bead events, cost ledger, escalation summary, evidence directories), and it owns operator policy (`--harness`/`--model`/`--profile`/`--max-cost`/`--no-review`/`--escalate`). It does **not** need to own provider/harness/cost/local-first routing — agent SDK already does that.

| Concern | DDx (now, post-87fb72c2) | Agent SDK |
|---|---|---|
| Profile selection | `RouteRequest.Profile` straight to agent | `default/cheap/standard/smart/local/offline/air-gapped/fast/code-smart/code-high` |
| Per-tier model pinning | opt-in via `--override-model` | `RouteRequest.Model` |
| Escalation on no-provider | opt-in via `--escalate` (CLI path); **not yet on server worker path** | `escalateProfileLadder` walks `ProfileEscalationLadder=[cheap,standard,smart]` on `ErrNoLiveProvider` |
| Cost-aware ranking | n/a (delegated) | `internal/routing/score.go` ranks by `CostClass` (local=0 wins) |
| Adaptive success-rate gating | `escalation.AdaptiveMinTier` (sticky 50-window cheap-tier lockout, only consumed via `--escalate`) | `routing.Inputs.HistoricalSuccess map[harness]float64` |
| Decision trace | bead events `kind:tier-attempt` per RouteCandidate | `RouteDecision.Candidates []RouteCandidate` |

## Optional follow-ups (not in this epic — file separately if/when needed)

These are no longer blockers; track separately if pursued:

- **Default profile = `local`** so the operator-default honors the local-LLM-first goal without an explicit `--profile local`. Currently default is `default` which maps via project config.
- **Inject `routing.Inputs.HistoricalSuccess` per-harness** to replace `escalation.AdaptiveMinTier` with agent-native success-rate routing. Frees DDx from maintaining its own success-window state machine.
- **Delete `cli/internal/agent/profile_ladder.go` helpers** once no caller (CLI or server) consumes them. Currently consumed only via `--escalate`; if `--escalate` is dropped or moved into agent, these can go.
- **Delete `cli/internal/escalation/escalation.go::AdaptiveMinTier`** once the HistoricalSuccess injection above is in place.

## Out of scope for this epic

- Changes to agent SDK source — if a gap is found, file an upstream bead under `~/Projects/agent/.ddx/`.
- UI/e2e work — the parent e2e drift epic `ddx-ccdf9cf9` covers that and is independent.
- Server source changes outside `cli/internal/server/workers.go` — no API endpoint changes, no GraphQL schema changes.","events_attachment":"ddx-3f406266/events.jsonl","id":"ddx-3f406266","issue_type":"epic","labels":["area:routing","area:agent","kind:refactor","phase:build"],"notes":"REVIEW:BLOCK

Diff contains only execution evidence files (manifest.json, result.json) — no source changes to cli/internal/server/workers.go or anywhere else. None of the AC items can be verified from this diff.","priority":1,"status":"closed","title":"routing-unify: delegate provider/harness/cost/local-first routing to agent SDK; ddx preserves execution-unit metadata + operator policy","updated_at":"2026-04-29T18:30:06.928832371Z"}

The autorouting epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on any of them for its exit gate; they are Phase 2 tracked separately.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"abeea29135139fae380daa51b3bb6df51e83395d","created_at":"2026-04-18T01:14:30.750496873Z","description":"## Problem (filed per codex + opus fresh-eyes review 2026-04-17)

Both reviews flagged the same missing concept: routing currently scores candidates on cost class, quota, and historical success rate — but does NOT gate on whether the resolved route can actually execute the requested work. Concrete failure modes this lets through:

1. **Context-window overflow.** Bead prompt is 80k tokens. Fuzzy match picks a 32k-context model on the pinned provider. Routing 'succeeds'. Dispatch fails mid-stream with a provider 400 or truncated output.

2. **Missing tool-calling support.** Some omlx flavors or older local servers don't implement /v1/chat/completions with tools. Routing picks them via fuzzy match. Dispatch fails with 'tools not supported' or silently returns a no-tool response that the execute-bead loop can't use.

3. **Missing structured-output support.** Some provider/model combos don't implement JSON-mode / tool-use-required. If the bead prompt relies on it, routing-level blindness means the failure only surfaces at dispatch.

4. **Effort / permissions.** --effort high or --permissions unrestricted may not be honored by every harness+provider combination. Routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and fail.

Codex: 'routing currently scores mostly on cost/quota/history, not whether a provider/model can satisfy tool calling, effort controls, permissions, or context-window needs.'
Opus: 'If the bead prompt is 80k tokens and the fuzzy-matched model is 32k, routing succeeds and dispatch fails mid-stream. Omlx flavor may not support tools at all.'

## Out of scope for this bead

This bead tracks the CONCEPT as a sibling of the main autorouting epic, but a full capability-constraint scoring system is a larger change than the current Phase-1 scope. Per opus: 'Scope this out of the epic explicitly (don't expand), but add a sibling bead so it's tracked.'

Split out into sub-scope bites is TBD:

1. **Context-window gating** — smallest useful slice. RouteRequest gains an optional EstimatedPromptTokens field; evaluateCandidate rejects (or penalizes) candidates whose concrete_model's known context window is below that. Uses v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits + catalog ContextWindow fallback.

2. **Tool-calling support gating** — needs per-provider / per-flavor capability metadata. Likely populated from discovery (/v1/models/status) + hand-curated capability table for harnesses.

3. **Effort / permissions gating** — mostly already in place for harnesses (Harness.EffortFlag checked at routing.go:rejectIfNotViable) but provider-specific gating (e.g., 'openrouter doesn't honor effort on non-reasoning models') is not.

4. **Structured-output support** — probably lowest priority; most reasoning-tool-capable models also do structured output.

This bead's AC is to file the sub-beads (smaller, focused) and close THIS bead once the sub-beads exist. The epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on those sub-beads for its exit gate — context-window etc. are a Phase 2 concern. The epic is about correct resolution; capability gating is about preventing resolved-but-unrunnable routes.

## Files touched (by sub-beads, not this one)

- cli/internal/agent/types.go — CandidatePlan capability fields, RouteRequest capability hints
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — capability checks in rejectIfNotViable + scoring
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go — ContextWindow, supports_tools, supports_structured_output per ModelEntry/Surface","events_attachment":"ddx-4817edfd/events.jsonl","execute-loop-no-changes-count":1,"id":"ddx-4817edfd","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:implementation","area:agent","area:routing"],"notes":"Decomposed into sub-beads (2026-04-18, in execution worktree 20260418T064927-78e29296):

- .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-f5cec42a — context-window gating (P2)
- .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-854cb8a7 — tool-calling support gating (P2)
- .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-bb230ad5 — effort/permissions gating (P3)
- .execute-bead-wt-ddx-4817edfd-20260418T064927-78e29296-22cadb21 — structured-output gating (P4)

All four sub-beads have parent=ddx-4817edfd. Each is prioritized per the parent description (context-window first as the smallest useful slice; structured-output last since it is likely covered incidentally by tool-calling gating). Per AC#5, the autorouting epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on any of these for its exit gate; they are tracked as Phase 2 work.

Note on bead IDs: the sub-beads picked up the worktree directory basename as their ID prefix instead of the conventional ddx- namespace. This is an upstream wart in `ddx bead create` ID generation when invoked from inside an execute-bead worktree (cwd basename leaks into the seq prefix). The beads themselves are correct — only the IDs are cosmetic. File a separate bead against the ddx CLI if this needs to be fixed.","parent":"ddx-2d974641","priority":2,"session_id":"eb-665c47aa","spec-id":"SD-015","status":"closed","title":"routing: pre-dispatch capability check — context window, tool support, effort, permissions","updated_at":"2026-04-18T06:56:44.667529862Z"}

- Replace markdown section headings in the synthesized execute-bead prompt with XML-style tags for machine-significant structure
- Preserve the current human-readable content while making the prompt easier to parse, diff, and validate deterministically
- Define which sections are required, optional, and repeatable in the prompt template
- Keep the prompt aligned with the tracked execution manifest and future commit-provenance rendering
- Update the governing specs and contracts so the prompt-template contract is documented rather than only implied by code

## Required spec work
- Update FEAT-006 and any applicable execute-bead contract docs to define the prompt-template structure
- Align the execution-evidence lane docs so prompt.md is explicitly structured, machine-readable evidence

The execute-bead prompt template tells the agent to commit:
  '4. Commit once, when everything is green. Stage only the files you intentionally changed with git add <specific-paths>...'

So agent self-commit is the contract. But:

(a) When typecheck/lint/test fails, the agent is told to fix or write no_changes_rationale.txt — but apparently many agents are bailing silently without writing the rationale either, so the harness sees a clean worktree (no diff staged or committed) and treats it as 'agent made no commits'. The iteration then carries only the auto-generated execution evidence.

(b) There may also be a path where the agent commits but a pre-commit hook silently rejects it. Have not confirmed; the worktree was reaped before I could inspect.

The issue spans multiple harnesses (claude, codex via embedded openrouter), so it is not specific to one provider's commit semantics. Either:
  - The harness should reject 'agent made no commits AND no no_changes_rationale.txt' as a separate failure class instead of silently letting ddx-land-coordinator land an empty iteration
  - The agent prompt should make rationale-writing on incomplete work non-optional with stronger language
  - The harness should run a final-stage check 'did the agent produce ANY signal — commit OR rationale — and if not, treat as harness-execution-failed and surface to the operator'","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-30T12:43:25.451101407Z","id":"ddx-4e0a4a59","issue_type":"bug","labels":["area:agent","area:harness","area:execute-bead","kind:correctness"],"owner":"erik","priority":1,"status":"closed","title":"execute-bead: agents make edits but skip git commit; iteration commits land only execution evidence","updated_at":"2026-04-30T12:43:46.370829758Z"}

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:282:{"acceptance":"The DDx docs define `ddx agent execute-bead` as a clean-worktree workspace transform from `W` to `W'`; the bead is specified as the executable transform contract rather than a vague tracker ticket; DDx docs no longer assign governing-artifact resolution or prompt construction to DDx at runtime; a generic bead execution-readiness validation surface is specified with clear DDx-owned vs HELIX-owned checks; the docs define the success/failure state machine including structural validation, required post-run checks, preserve-on-failure evidence, and rebase+fast-forward landing on success; and the contract explicitly remains valid for future multi-project `ddx server` worker pools rather than assuming a single served repo","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","created_at":"2026-04-09T22:00:19.106711764Z","description":"<context-digest>
Review area: execute-bead semantics, executable bead contracts, readiness validation, and DDx/HELIX boundary ownership. Evidence covers FEAT-006, FEAT-010, FEAT-013, SD-012, server-driven execution discussions, and cli/cmd/agent_execute_bead.go.

Define DDx bead execution around the functional model `W' = execute(bead, W)`, where `W` is a clean git-backed workspace input state and `ddx agent execute-bead` materializes that state in an isolated worktree before mutation.

## Required design outcomes
1. Clarify that `ddx agent execute-bead` starts from a resolved base revision, treats the caller's dirty worktree as outside the transform, checkpoints it separately, and executes only against the isolated worktree state.
2. Define the bead as the executable transform contract: the bead must carry the instruction/prompt, acceptance, declared context inputs, execution policy, landing/preservation policy, and post-run validation policy needed for replayable execution.
3. Unwind DDx designs that currently imply DDx derives governing artifacts, prompt construction, or workflow policy from the live document graph at execution time. DDx should provide composable graph/exec tools, not hidden workflow orchestration.
4. Define a generic DDx execution-readiness validation surface for beads, such as `ddx bead validate --execution` or equivalent inspection semantics, that checks structural executability without embedding HELIX-specific policy.
5. Specify the success/failure state machine around execution: structural validation, agent execution, post-run validity checks / required executions, preserve-on-failure evidence semantics, and land-on-success semantics.
6. Make the automation boundary explicit: future humans, HELIX flows, or `ddx server` worker pools must consume the explicit bead contract and validator output rather than relying on hidden prompt assembly or runtime artifact inference.
7. Preserve room for multi-project server execution: the contract must not assume a single served repo or a single server process, and must remain valid when project-scoped worktrees are managed by a multi-project `ddx server`.
8. Make HELIX's responsibility explicit: HELIX should author the executable bead contract and create a new contract document in `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/` that defines the DDx/HELIX boundary for executable bead execution. The contracts directory does not currently exist in this repo, so this work must establish it.

## Spec and design scope
- Reconcile FEAT-006 agent-service workflow with the workspace-transform model
- Reconcile FEAT-010 execution-definition language so execution selection and required checks are explicit input or separately composed query output, not hidden bead-context derivation
- Preserve FEAT-013's substrate-not-orchestration boundary while still supporting async server-managed worker pools
- Align SD-012 git-awareness wording with the actual clean-input/worktree model, checkpoint semantics, and preserve-vs-land outcomes
- Establish how the generic execution-readiness surface is consumed by future automation without embedding HELIX policy in DDx

## Expected artifact work
- New contract under `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/` for the executable bead boundary
- Updates to the affected feature / solution design docs so they all describe the same execution contract
- Follow-on design beads for server-managed supervision and multi-project server topology
- Follow-on implementation beads only after the contract is settled","execution-eligible":true,"id":"ddx-5448b39c","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:planning","kind:architecture","area:agent","area:bead","area:docs"],"priority":0,"spec-id":"FEAT-006","status":"closed","title":"Define execute-bead workspace-transform contract and readiness validation","updated_at":"2026-04-10T16:14:55.073044325Z"}

AC2. `ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --no-adaptive-min-tier` succeeds against a fresh project (no model_overrides in .ddx/config.yaml) when the catalog has at least one tier with a reachable harness/model/provider chain.

AC4. `ddx agent doctor` (or `ddx agent catalog show`) emits a warning when a tier's catalog entries reference a model id that has no matching named route AND no provider /v1/models entry across configured providers, so the user discovers the gap before invoking execute-loop.

Per-project agent.routing.model_overrides remains a supported override that wins over catalog defaults — backwards compatible.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-29T04:14:56Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"131811","closing_commit_sha":"351dc52260be4f5d45484fc8a8d77500fb35f48b","created_at":"2026-04-29T03:28:05.434417294Z","description":"When agent.routing.model_overrides is not set in a project's .ddx/config.yaml, ResolveTierModelRef in cli/internal/agent/profile_ladder.go:44 returns the literal tier name ("cheap", "standard", "smart") as the model identifier. ResolveRoute is then called with model="cheap"/"standard"/"smart" — strings that match no model id and no named route. Every tier resolves to an empty harness/empty model and is recorded as a tier-attempt with body "no viable harness found". The loop exhausts all tiers and reports "execute-loop: all tiers exhausted — no viable provider found".

This is happening today on a workstation that has bragi/vidar/hel hosting qwen3.6, plus working claude and codex harnesses. execute-bead succeeds with the same providers when --harness/--model are pinned explicitly, proving the underlying routing is fine. Only the tier→model resolution path is broken.

The model catalog (~/.ddx/model-catalog.yaml or built-in defaults, surfaced via `ddx agent catalog show`) ALREADY contains the tier→surface→model mapping that would make this work. The resolver just doesn't consume it. Surfaces and concrete models are listed per tier (e.g. cheap: embedded-openai→qwen3.5-27b, codex→gpt-5.4-mini, claude→claude-haiku-4-5) — the resolver should walk these surfaces in order, picking the first whose harness is healthy and whose model resolves to a reachable provider via model_routes. Per-project model_overrides should be an OPTIONAL override layered on top of catalog defaults, not the only thing that makes tier resolution work.

Caller-side workaround: every project must add agent.routing.model_overrides to .ddx/config.yaml before execute-loop will function. This is silent: there is no diagnostic from `ddx agent doctor`, no warning from `ddx agent catalog show`, and no hint in the "no viable provider" error pointing at the missing config. Users have to read source to understand why a fully-healthy provider/harness fleet produces zero viable tiers.

Repro:
1. Fresh project with .ddx/config.yaml lacking agent.routing.model_overrides
2. Healthy provider config in ~/.config/agent/config.yaml (any qwen/claude/codex)
3. `ddx agent execute-loop --once --local --no-adaptive-min-tier`
4. Every tier-attempt event records "no viable harness found"; loop exits with 'all tiers exhausted'

Related symptom — the adaptive min-tier promotion uses cheap-tier success rate to skip cheap, but with the bug above no cheap-tier attempt has ever produced a successful harness invocation, so the trailing window is biased to 0% by design rather than by actual model performance.","events_attachment":"ddx-5538aa5b/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-29T04:40:26.540559641Z","id":"ddx-5538aa5b","issue_type":"bug","labels":["area:agent","area:routing","kind:design-flaw"],"owner":"ddx","priority":1,"session_id":"eb-f61475d0","status":"closed","title":"execute-loop: tier resolver should consume catalog instead of requiring per-project model_overrides","updated_at":"2026-04-29T04:41:11.568927737Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:286:{"acceptance":"Concurrent execute-bead workers no longer checkpoint unrelated DDx runtime churn from other active attempts into their base revisions; the snapshot boundary for DDx-owned files is documented and enforced; and concurrency tests cover the reproduced multi-worker case.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","closing_commit_sha":"85513f47896eaa63d7a9feaeda4318867131d6aa","created_at":"2026-04-11T04:05:59.944262787Z","description":"<context-digest>
Observed while running multiple execute-loop workers concurrently on 2026-04-11. Replacing git stash with checkpoint commits fixed the immediate beads.lock/workers race, but execute-bead still starts from a dirty repo root where .ddx/beads.jsonl and other DDx-owned execution artifacts may be changing because of other live workers. That means one attempt can inherit unrelated DDx runtime/evidence churn into its checkpoint base.
</context-digest>
Tighten execute-bead checkpointing so an attempt's input snapshot is not polluted by unrelated DDx-generated state from other live executions.

## Scope
- Define which DDx-owned paths are part of the execution input snapshot vs excluded runtime churn
- Prevent unrelated live tracker/evidence/worker mutations from being captured into a new attempt base by default
- Add concurrency-focused tests covering simultaneous workers and dirty DDx-owned state
- Keep the tracked execution-evidence contract coherent with the new snapshot boundary","events_attachment":"ddx-55f05e07/events.jsonl","id":"ddx-55f05e07","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:correctness","area:agent","area:exec","area:git","area:cli"],"parent":"ddx-32b3008e","priority":0,"status":"closed","title":"Keep execute-bead checkpoint bases free of unrelated DDx runtime churn","updated_at":"2026-04-11T05:29:29.156293566Z"}
.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:289:{"archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-11T19:53:03Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"2860272","created_at":"2026-04-11T19:33:05.943045249Z","description":"ddx agent execute-loop fails on eitri: agent harness routes to z-ai/glm-5.1 ignoring local vidar config

ddx agent execute-loop consistently fails on the helix machine (eitri) with provider-level errors.
The execute-loop correctly selects and claims beads, but the agent harness never runs the work.
CRITICAL: ddx agent run --harness agent works perfectly on the same machine, pointing to a
model-resolution bug specifically in execute-bead/execute-loop.

PROOF: direct agent run works, execute-loop fails:
  ddx agent run --text "Hello" --harness agent --timeout 60s → succeeds with qwen3.5-27b on vidar
  ddx agent execute-loop --once --json --harness agent → fails with z-ai/glm-5.1 via OpenRouter 404

ROOT CAUSE: Model resolution in execute-bead injects --model z-ai/glm-5.1, overriding the
native ~/.config/agent/config.yaml provider config (default_provider: vidar, model: qwen3.5-27b).
The z-ai/glm-5.1 model is not defined in models.yaml or the native config — it appears to come
from stale execute-bead routing metadata. Even explicit --model flags on execute-loop don't
propagate correctly to the spawned execute-bead subprocess.

PROVIDER STATUS on eitri:
- vidar (http://vidar:1234/v1, qwen3.5-27b): WORKS — confirmed with ddx agent run
- bragi (http://bragi:1234/v1, qwen3.5-27b): DOWN — connection refused
- grendel (http://grendel:1234/v1, qwen3.5-27b): DOWN — connection refused
- openrouter (https://openrouter.ai/api/v1): FAILS — 404 guardrail restriction on claude-haiku

MACHINE CONTEXT (eitri, Linux):
- Hostname: eitri, paths use /home/erik/ and linuxbrew
- ~/.config/agent/config.yaml defines providers with default_provider: vidar
- models.yaml defines code-high profile → agent.openai: gpt-5.4 (OpenRouter surface)
- No ~/.ddx.yml config file
- No project-level .agent/config.yaml
- Codex and claude harnesses nearly out of quota — reserved for review, not bulk execution
- execute-loop was working previously (existing ddx beads show successful runs)
- z-ai/glm-5.1 string appears nowhere in source — comes from runtime routing metadata

ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES: No AGENT_PROVIDER, AGENT_BASE_URL, AGENT_API_KEY, or AGENT_MODEL set.
All routing comes from native ~/.config/agent/config.yaml.

IMPACT: ddx agent execute-loop is the primary execution surface for HELIX beads. If the only
working provider (vidar → qwen3.5-27b) cannot be used from execute-loop, entire queues drain
as failures. The loop records each with retry_after (6 hours) and retries indefinitely.

SUGGESTED FIXES:
1. Fix execute-bead model resolution: respect native .agent/config.yaml provider config or
   properly propagate the --model flag from execute-loop → execute-bead
2. Add provider health checks: ddx agent doctor should test the actual provider with a
   minimal API call, not just check binary existence
3. Fail open on provider errors: when the default provider fails, fall back to the next
   available provider in config rather than hard-failing

.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl:297:{"acceptance":"- [ ] Schema added to .ddx/config.yaml for executions.mirror (kind, path, include, async)
- [ ] At least one mirror backend implemented (local-dir is the minimum; s3 preferred)
- [ ] execute-bead finalization triggers an async mirror upload that does not block bead completion
- [ ] mirror-index.jsonl appended with attempt_id, bead_id, mirror_uri, bytes, timestamp
- [ ] Mirror failures logged but do not fail the bead
- [ ] `ddx agent executions fetch <attempt_id>` retrieves a mirrored bundle back to local disk
- [ ] Path template supports {project}, {attempt_id}, {date}, {bead_id}
- [ ] Default config includes the full bundle (embedded included)
- [ ] Integration test: local-dir mirror roundtrip (run execute-bead stub, assert bundle mirrored, fetch it back, bytes match)
- [ ] Docs: update TD-010 (or add TD-011) with the mirror design and operator runbook","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-18T06:17:17Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1833233","closing_commit_sha":"d810ddff35013426b0e3ec92f4e1ad1fcb45c777","created_at":"2026-04-15T18:59:41.811906799Z","description":"Execution bundles under .ddx/executions/<attempt>/ are the primary forensic record of what DDx automation did, but the most valuable part — the per-iteration agent trace at embedded/agent-*.jsonl — cannot reasonably be checked in. Individual traces can exceed 50 MB; a busy automation day produces hundreds of MB. Axon hit this on 2026-04-15 when GitHub warned about a 50.18 MB embedded trace file, and embedded/ is now gitignored per-repo.

We still want that level of detail for *every* run, for post-mortems, routing/model analysis, and training-data curation. We need a configurable out-of-band mirror.

## Proposed shape

1. **Archive target** configured via `.ddx/config.yaml` (and/or env var):
   - `executions.mirror.kind: local | s3 | gcs | http`
   - `executions.mirror.path: s3://bucket/ddx-executions/{project}/{attempt_id}/` (template with `{project}`, `{attempt_id}`, `{date}`, `{bead_id}`)
   - `executions.mirror.include: [manifest, prompt, result, usage, checks, embedded]` (allow excluding embedded for bandwidth-sensitive setups, but default = everything)
   - `executions.mirror.async: true` (upload in background after execute-bead returns so it never blocks the hot path)

2. **When to mirror**: at execute-bead finalization, after result.json is written. On failure, retry async with bounded attempts; log failures to `.ddx/agent-logs/mirror.log` but do not fail the bead.

3. **Index**: each mirrored bundle gets a pointer row appended to a local `.ddx/executions/mirror-index.jsonl` (attempt_id, bead_id, mirror_uri, uploaded_at, byte_size). This gives analysts a quick `jq` lookup without walking the remote store.

4. **Retrieval**: `ddx agent executions fetch <attempt_id>` pulls a bundle back to .ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/ for local inspection.

5. **GC**: optional local retention policy (`executions.retain_days`) that prunes old local bundles while the mirror keeps the full history.

## Non-goals

- Not a full audit trail service — just a mirror of DDx's existing bundles.
- Not a replacement for git-tracked manifest/prompt/result/usage summaries — those stay checked in.
- Not a distributed log aggregator — one-shot copy per bundle, no streaming.

## Why now

Axon's ADR-018 session produced ~85 MB of execution artifacts in a single day; only 1.5 MB of that is useful as checked-in summary. Without a mirror, every project using DDx execute-bead faces the same tension: either gitignore the traces and lose the forensic data, or commit them and bloat the repo forever.","events_attachment":"ddx-5930ed71/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-04-18T06:37:17.253079303Z","execute-loop-last-detail":"execution_failed","execute-loop-last-status":"execution_failed","execute-loop-retry-after":"","id":"ddx-5930ed71","issue_type":"task","labels":["ddx","area:agent","area:observability","area:storage","kind:feature"],"owner":"erik","priority":2,"session_id":"eb-312c9be3","status":"closed","title":"execution-bundle archive: configurable mirror for .ddx/executions/ (including embedded traces)","updated_at":"2026-04-18T06:37:50.507998511Z"}

Regression test: two workers on two different beads complete in arbitrary order; parent process merges both sequentially with interactive conflict resolution if needed; no worktree-internal merge code paths are exercised.","archived_at":"2026-05-02T04:37:57Z","claimed-at":"2026-04-14T05:36:33Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"1412283","closing_commit_sha":"49c3d2bda576630dd2f6555bc143082344f079ba","created_at":"2026-04-14T04:02:25.941816183Z","description":"The current execute-bead worker does WAY too much: creates a worktree, runs the agent, evaluates required gates, decides merge-by-default vs preserve vs land-conflict, writes preserve refs, synthesizes commits on IsDirty, classifies 7+ outcomes, manages heartbeats, handles claim/unclaim contention, runs recoverOrphans on stale worktrees. This surface is why (a) two concurrent workers race on the main-branch merge and the loser's good work ends up under refs/ddx/iterations/ needing manual rescue, (b) no_changes gets misclassified as synthesized-commit success, (c) the gate-enforcement path and the merge path are entangled, and (d) the worker needs special privileges (update-ref, merge, rebase) that a simple task-runner should never have.\n\nThe correct design is the one the operator just described: execute-bead is a thin worker. 'I have been handed a worktree and a task. I will execute that task and make commits in the worktree. I will stream progress events to ddx server. I will notify ddx server when I am done, reporting the worktree branch and the commit(s) I made. Then I exit.' That's it.\n\nEverything else — merge to main, conflict resolution, required-gate evaluation, preserve-under-ref, decide whether to close the bead — moves to the PARENT process (the operator, or a driver tool running interactively in the terminal). Merge conflicts are resolved interactively the same as any other merge. A human (or an outer agent with full git authority) reviews the agent's commits and decides what to do with them.\n\nThis simplification deletes most of ExecuteBead() in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go and moves:\n- resolveBase / baseRev tracking → parent\n- Merge() call → parent\n- preserveRef / UpdateRef / refs/ddx/iterations/ writes → parent\n- evaluateRequiredGates → parent (or a separate 'ddx agent check' step)\n- outcome classification 'merged/preserved/land-conflict/error' → parent\n- recoverOrphans → parent (cleanup runs when parent next launches workers)\n- heartbeatIsStale reclaim logic → parent (parent tracks wot` for Go. The bead description will usually name the exact commands. **Do not commit red code.** If anything fails, diagnose and fix it before committing.

5. **Commit exactly once.** Use `git add <specific-paths>` with the file paths you actually changed — never `git add -A`, which can pick up unrelated files in the worktree. Use a conventional-commit subject ending with `[<bead-id>]`. Commit implementation and tests in the same commit so the reviewer sees them together.

6. **Stop after the commit succeeds.** Return immediately. Do not continue reading files, running extra tests, or asking yourself follow-up questions — the work is done, and returning promptly is how execute-bead signals success to the orchestrator. Continuing past the commit risks runaway loops.

## Quality bar and the review step

After your commit merges, an automated review step may check your work against the acceptance criteria. If any AC item is unmet, the bead reopens and escalates to a higher-capability model, and the review findings are threaded into the next attempt's prompt as a `<review-findings>` section. **The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet the AC in this pass.**

If this prompt already contains a `<review-findings>` section, every BLOCKING finding in it is a concrete thing the previous attempt missed. Address each one before declaring the work complete — do not declare no_changes with blocking findings still unaddressed.

## The bead contract overrides project defaults

The bead description and AC override any CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or project-level conservative defaults in this worktree. If the bead asks for new files, write them. Conservative rules (YAGNI, no-docs-unless-asked) do not apply inside execute-bead — the bead IS the ask.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — it corrupts the bead queue.
- Do not touch CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or any other project-instructions file unless the bead explicitly asks for it.
- Stage only the files you intentionally changed.

.ddx/executions/20260418T212659-278a6f4c/prompt.md:17:You are a coding agent executing one bead inside an isolated DDx execution worktree. You have tools: read, write, edit, bash, ls, grep, find. Use them directly — do not shell out to cat/tail/rg/find; use the tools.

## Process

1. **Read first.** Before writing any code, read the files the bead description names. If the description says "A1 landed X at <path>", read that path. If it references a spec section, read that section. Do NOT start editing without seeing the existing code — this is how you avoid making the same change twice or breaking an invariant you did not know about.

2. **Plan the work briefly.** Internally note which files you will touch, which tests you will add, and which verification commands you will run. Keep the plan short — one or two sentences.

3. **Implement.** Use `edit` for targeted changes to existing files and `write` for brand-new files. Use `read` (not `bash: cat`) to read files. Use `grep` (not `bash: rg`) to search. Use `ls` (not `bash: ls`) for directory listings.

4. **Verify before committing.** Run the project's test and lint commands — typically `cargo test -p <crate>` and `cargo clippy -p <crate> -- -D warnings` for Rust, `bun test` for JS/TS, `go test` for Go. The bead's <description> and <acceptance> sections above are the completion contract. Every AC checkbox must be satisfied by code you write in this pass.

## Process

1. **Read first.** Before writing any code, read the files the bead description names. If the description says "A1 landed X at <path>", read that path. If it references a spec section, read that section. Do NOT start editing without seeing the existing code — this is how you avoid making the same change twice or breaking an invariant you did not know about.

2. **Plan the work briefly.** Internally note which files you will touch, which tests you will add, and which verification commands you will run. Keep the plan short — one or two sentences.

3. **Implement.** Use `edit` for targeted changes to existing files and `write` for brand-new files. Use `read` (not `bash: cat`) to read files. Use `grep` (not `bash: rg`) to search. Use `ls` (not `bash: ls`) for directory listings.

4. **Verify before committing.** Run the project's test and lint commands — typically `cargo test -p <crate>` and `cargo clippy -p <crate> -- -D warnings` for Rust, `bun test` for JS/TS, `go test` for Go. The bead description will usually name the exact commands. **Do not commit red code.** If anything fails, diagnose and fix it before committing.

5. **Commit exactly once.** Use `git add <specific-paths>` with the file paths you actually changed — never `git add -A`, which can pick up unrelated files in the worktree. Use a conventional-commit subject ending with `[<bead-id>]`. Commit implementation and tests in the same commit so the reviewer sees them together.

6. **Stop after the commit succeeds.** Return immediately. Do not continue reading files, running extra tests, or asking yourself follow-up questions — the work is done, and returning promptly is how execute-bead signals success to the orchestrator. Continuing past the commit risks runaway loops.

## If you cannot finish

Write your reasoning to `.ddx/executions/20260418T005009-71d5356c/no_changes_rationale.txt` with: (a) what is done, (b) what is blocking, (c) what a follow-up would need. Do NOT commit partial or red code — a well-justified no_changes is better than a bad commit. The bead will be re-queued for another attempt.

## Quality bar and the review step

After your commit merges, an automated review step may check your work against the acceptance criteria. If any AC item is unmet, the bead reopens and escalates to a higher-capability model, and the review findings are threaded into the next attempt's prompt as a `<review-findings>` section. **The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet the AC in this pass.**

If this prompt already contains a `<review-findings>` section, every BLOCKING finding in it is a concrete thing the previous attempt missed. Address each one before declaring the work complete — do not declare no_changes with blocking findings still unaddressed.

## The bead contract overrides project defaults

The bead description and AC override any CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or project-level conservative defaults in this worktree. If the bead asks for new files, write them. Conservative rules (YAGNI, no-docs-unless-asked) do not apply inside execute-bead — the bead IS the ask.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — it corrupts the bead queue.
- Do not touch CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or any other project-instructions file unless the bead explicitly asks for it.
- Stage only the files you intentionally changed.

.ddx/executions/20260416T180825-2aaff03d/manifest.json:14:    "description": "## Scope

execute-bead's orchestrator currently leaves the per-attempt evidence files it
materializes (`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/manifest.json`, `prompt.md`,
`result.json`, `checks.json`, `usage.json`) untracked after the agent run
returns. Successful bead commits reach main via the "Merge bead" path, but the
evidence sits in the working tree unstaged and orphaned. This bead closes that
gap: after the agent finishes, DDx (not the agent) stages the evidence and
returns the working tree clean.

Concrete evidence of the gap: commit 98e379b on main is a one-shot backfill
that imports 254 historical `.ddx/executions/<ts>-<hash>/` directories
(2026-04-11 → 2026-04-15) that were never staged by the execute-bead pipeline.
That backfill must not need to happen again.

## Files to Touch

- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go` — the land/merge flow. After the
  agent's commit is produced (or after `Merge()` / bare-ref update), and
  before returning `ExecuteBeadReport`, stage the per-attempt evidence dir and
  fold it into the merge. Two shapes are acceptable:
  (a) amend the evidence into the bead's own commit (agent's commit + DDx's
      evidence files, single commit), OR
  (b) emit a trailing `chore: add execution evidence [<attempt-id>]` commit
      on the working branch as a sibling to the bead's commit, in the same
      merge transaction.
  Choose (b) if amending the agent's commit would rewrite its SHA — the agent
  commit's SHA is referenced by `session_id` / `closing_commit_sha` and must
  not move. Choose (a) only if the evidence can be staged BEFORE the agent
  commits (e.g. the agent is told via `git add` hooks to pick up a DDx-seeded
  index). Pragmatic recommendation: (b).
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go` — the per-run orchestrator entry. After
  the executor returns, verify the working tree is clean; if it is not,
  diagnose untracked files introduced by this run, stage them into the
  trailing evidence commit (if they are under `.ddx/executions/<attempt>/`),
  and fail the run with a clear error otherwise ("orchestrator left worktree
  dirty: <path>").
- `cli/internal/server/workers.go` — the server-worker path (`runWorker`,
  `buildAgentRunner`). Same contract applies: after `ExecuteBead` returns and
  `LandBeadResult` is called, the worker must verify the worktree is clean
  before declaring the attempt complete. If the land flow is still dropping
  evidence, surface that as a test failure, not a silent leak.
- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land_test.go` OR a new
  `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_evidence_test.go` — regression tests.

## Files NOT to Touch

- `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go` lines 875-960 — the per-bead
  instructions text (`executeBeadInstructionsClaudeText` /
  `executeBeadInstructionsAgentText`). Those are agent-facing and must keep
  telling the agent to `git add <specific-paths>` only. The fix belongs in
  the orchestrator, not the prompt: DDx wrote the evidence, DDx commits it.
- `AGENTS.md`, `docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-agent-service.md`,
  `skills/ddx-bead/SKILL.md` — the policy is already correct as of commit
  3a2a0c5. This bead implements the policy; it does not amend it.

## Cross-Bead Context

- Parent epic `ddx-32b3008e` ("Evolve execute-bead into a tracked
  execution-evidence workflow", P0, spec-id FEAT-006) is the meta for this
  work. This bead is the first execution-layer task under that epic.
- The trail-preservation policy is codified in commit 3a2a0c5: AGENTS.md
  "Merge Policy" section, FEAT-006 "Execution Trail Preservation in
  Downstream Merges" section (with 3 acceptance criteria), and
  skills/ddx-bead/SKILL.md "Branch Merge Policy" section.
- The land flow today uses a mix of `git merge --no-ff` and
  `git update-ref` (for bare-repo worktrees) — see
  cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go:166-544 for the three code paths
  that touch the merge. Any of them may be the right insertion point; pick
  the one that runs LAST after the agent commit is finalized.
- The bug-fix commit 2792dfc ("anchor SessionLogDir to project root") gives
  the orchestrator a reliable absolute path for session logs. This bead is
  the analogous fix for execution evidence.

## Spec Excerpt (FEAT-006 Execution Trail Preservation, normative)

> **Execution Trail Preservation in Downstream Merges** [...]
> Bead records store `closing_commit_sha` as a pointer back into git
> history; routing, cost, latency, retry-count, and tier-escalation reports
> read these commits directly. The trail realizes the `output = bead(input)`
> contract the system is built on: every bead attempt is recoverable from
> git history alone.
>
> **Acceptance Criteria (execution trail preservation):**
> - Given a branch containing at least one `chore: update tracker
>   (execute-bead` commit, when the branch is merged to its target, then
>   every commit SHA on the feature branch remains reachable from the target
>   branch HEAD after the merge.
> - Given a bead has `closing_commit_sha` set, when the branch containing
>   that commit is merged to main, then `git cat-file -e
>   <closing_commit_sha>` still succeeds on main after the merge.

This bead adds a fourth, implementation-layer criterion: that the
per-attempt evidence dir is *present in the trail at all*, not merely
preserved across merges.

## Verification Commands

- `cd cli && go test ./internal/agent -run TestExecuteBeadLandsEvidence`
  passes (new regression test, see AC below).
- `cd cli && go test ./internal/agent ./internal/server ./cmd` passes
  (full suite; no regressions).
- Manual dogfooding: in a throwaway clone, run
  `ddx agent execute-loop --once` against a ready bead. After it returns,
  `git status --porcelain` MUST print nothing. `git log -1 --stat` MUST show
  at least one file under `.ddx/executions/<attempt>/` in the HEAD commit
  (or its immediate predecessor in the same merge).",

The bead description will usually name the exact commands. **Do not commit red code.** If anything fails, diagnose and fix it before committing.

5. **Commit exactly once.** Use `git add <specific-paths>` with the file paths you actually changed — never `git add -A`, which can pick up unrelated files in the worktree. Use a conventional-commit subject ending with `[<bead-id>]`. Commit implementation and tests in the same commit so the reviewer sees them together.

## Spec Excerpt (FEAT-006 Execution Trail Preservation, normative)

> **Execution Trail Preservation in Downstream Merges** [...]
> Bead records store `closing_commit_sha` as a pointer back into git
> history; routing, cost, latency, retry-count, and tier-escalation reports
> read these commits directly. The trail realizes the `output = bead(input)`
> contract the system is built on: every bead attempt is recoverable from
> git history alone.
>
> **Acceptance Criteria (execution trail preservation):**
> - Given a branch containing at least one `chore: update tracker
>   (execute-bead` commit, when the branch is merged to its target, then
>   every commit SHA on the feature branch remains reachable from the target
>   branch HEAD after the merge.
> - Given a bead has `closing_commit_sha` set, when the branch containing
>   that commit is merged to main, then `git cat-file -e
>   <closing_commit_sha>` still succeeds on main after the merge.

This bead adds a fourth, implementation-layer criterion: that the
per-attempt evidence dir is *present in the trail at all*, not merely
preserved across merges.

.ddx/executions/20260418T063725-f214140b/prompt.md:79:+    "description": "Execution bundles under .ddx/executions/<attempt>/ are the primary forensic record of what DDx automation did, but the most valuable part — the per-iteration agent trace at embedded/agent-*.jsonl — cannot reasonably be checked in. Individual traces can exceed 50 MB; a busy automation day produces hundreds of MB. Axon hit this on 2026-04-15 when GitHub warned about a 50.18 MB embedded trace file, and embedded/ is now gitignored per-repo.

We still want that level of detail for *every* run, for post-mortems, routing/model analysis, and training-data curation. We need a configurable out-of-band mirror.

## Proposed shape

1. **Archive target** configured via `.ddx/config.yaml` (and/or env var):
   - `executions.mirror.kind: local | s3 | gcs | http`
   - `executions.mirror.path: s3://bucket/ddx-executions/{project}/{attempt_id}/` (template with `{project}`, `{attempt_id}`, `{date}`, `{bead_id}`)
   - `executions.mirror.include: [manifest, prompt, result, usage, checks, embedded]` (allow excluding embedded for bandwidth-sensitive setups, but default = everything)
   - `executions.mirror.async: true` (upload in background after execute-bead returns so it never blocks the hot path)

2. **When to mirror**: at execute-bead finalization, after result.json is written. On failure, retry async with bounded attempts; log failures to `.ddx/agent-logs/mirror.log` but do not fail the bead.

3. **Index**: each mirrored bundle gets a pointer row appended to a local `.ddx/executions/mirror-index.jsonl` (attempt_id, bead_id, mirror_uri, uploaded_at, byte_size). This gives analysts a quick `jq` lookup without walking the remote store.

4. **Retrieval**: `ddx agent executions fetch <attempt_id>` pulls a bundle back to .ddx/executions/<attempt_id>/ for local inspection.

5. **GC**: optional local retention policy (`executions.retain_days`) that prunes old local bundles while the mirror keeps the full history.

## Non-goals

- Not a full audit trail service — just a mirror of DDx's existing bundles.
- Not a replacement for git-tracked manifest/prompt/result/usage summaries — those stay checked in.
- Not a distributed log aggregator — one-shot copy per bundle, no streaming.

## Why now

Axon's ADR-018 session produced ~85 MB of execution artifacts in a single day; only 1.5 MB of that is useful as checked-in summary. Without a mirror, every project using DDx execute-bead faces the same tension: either gitignore the traces and lose the forensic data, or commit them and bloat the repo forever.",

.ddx/executions/20260418T015654-977ef255/manifest.json:14:    "title": "agent: v0.3.11 omlx dispatch returns 'unexpected end of JSON input' under execute-bead prompts (direct curl works)",
.ddx/executions/20260418T015654-977ef255/manifest.json:15:    "description": "## Problem (re-scoped per fresh-eyes review 2026-04-17)

Running 'ddx work --once --local --provider vidar-omlx --model Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit --max-tier cheap' against an execute-bead prompt fails with:

    agent: provider error: unexpected end of JSON input

after 14.65s. No tokens consumed (input=0, output=0). The session log at .ddx/executions/20260418T005052-3aad2cfa/embedded/*.jsonl shows llm.delta err={Offset: 0} — parser read an empty/malformed first chunk.

## What the bead originally claimed (now disputed)

Original framing declared this an upstream ddx-agent v0.3.11 defect based on 'direct curl against the same endpoint works for non-streaming, tools, and SSE'. Both codex and opus fresh-eyes reviews pushed back that this attribution is not supported by the evidence:

- 'unexpected end of JSON input after 14s is exactly what you'd see if (a) the server closed the stream on an unsupported field, (b) DDx sent stream=true to an omlx flavor that returns a non-SSE 200 with empty body, or (c) the tool schema tripped server-side validation and the server returned {} + close.' — opus
- 'Routing and dispatch still have split control paths... That means malformed provider/model pairing is still possible before the request ever reaches the parser. unexpected end of JSON input is consistent with a broken upstream parser, but also with DDx sending a request shape or streaming mode the omlx server does not fully support.' — codex

## Fix — evidence first, attribution second

This bead now produces wire-level evidence BEFORE concluding where the defect lives. Acceptance criteria are scoped to that.

### Step 1: Capture the wire payload

Reproduce the failure on a host with vidar-omlx reachable and capture the exact HTTP request DDx sends and the exact HTTP response the server returns. Options in ascending order of preference:

a) Run with HTTPS_PROXY pointing at a local mitmproxy or a simple Go tcp-dump proxy that logs request/response bodies + headers to a file.
b) Add a temporary debug flag in ddx-agent (or ddx-side wrapper) to dump the request body + response headers + raw response bytes before parsing.
c) tcpdump/wireshark on the vidar host capturing port 1235, filtering to the DDx attempt's source IP and time window.

### Step 2: Classify the defect

With the wire payload in hand, determine which layer is at fault:

- **DDx bug**: request shape is inconsistent with what omlx (flavor-specific) accepts — e.g., stream=true on a server that returns non-SSE, tool schema shape mismatched, missing/extra header, etc. Fix in DDx (ddx-agent config or ddx-agent library usage).
- **ddx-agent bug**: request is valid and server returned valid SSE/JSON, but agent-lib's parser errored. Fix upstream; bump version when fixed.
- **omlx server bug**: request is valid and ddx-agent parser is correct, but server returned empty body or malformed SSE frames. Fix upstream at ml-explore/mlx_lm; work around in ddx-agent by detecting and retrying with different flags.

### Step 3: File and fix

Whichever of the three paths the evidence points to, file follow-up as needed (a targeted DDx fix, an agent-lib bump, or a workaround). This bead closes when the root cause is identified AND addressed AND a regression test or reproducer exists.

.ddx/executions/20260416T221105-f85ee523/prompt.md:5:## Scope

## Context
Local models (qwen3-coder-next, 32k context) frequently hit compaction limits on beads with large prompts. Shrinking the prompt for cheap-tier attempts lets them attempt more beads before escalating.

.ddx/executions/20260418T064927-78e29296/manifest.json:13:    "description": "## Problem (filed per codex + opus fresh-eyes review 2026-04-17)

Both reviews flagged the same missing concept: routing currently scores candidates on cost class, quota, and historical success rate — but does NOT gate on whether the resolved route can actually execute the requested work. Concrete failure modes this lets through:

1. **Context-window overflow.** Bead prompt is 80k tokens. Fuzzy match picks a 32k-context model on the pinned provider. Routing 'succeeds'. Dispatch fails mid-stream with a provider 400 or truncated output.

2. **Missing tool-calling support.** Some omlx flavors or older local servers don't implement /v1/chat/completions with tools. Routing picks them via fuzzy match. Dispatch fails with 'tools not supported' or silently returns a no-tool response that the execute-bead loop can't use.

3. **Missing structured-output support.** Some provider/model combos don't implement JSON-mode / tool-use-required. If the bead prompt relies on it, routing-level blindness means the failure only surfaces at dispatch.

4. **Effort / permissions.** --effort high or --permissions unrestricted may not be honored by every harness+provider combination. Routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and fail.

Codex: 'routing currently scores mostly on cost/quota/history, not whether a provider/model can satisfy tool calling, effort controls, permissions, or context-window needs.'
Opus: 'If the bead prompt is 80k tokens and the fuzzy-matched model is 32k, routing succeeds and dispatch fails mid-stream. Omlx flavor may not support tools at all.'

## Out of scope for this bead

This bead tracks the CONCEPT as a sibling of the main autorouting epic, but a full capability-constraint scoring system is a larger change than the current Phase-1 scope. Per opus: 'Scope this out of the epic explicitly (don't expand), but add a sibling bead so it's tracked.'

Split out into sub-scope bites is TBD:

1. **Context-window gating** — smallest useful slice. RouteRequest gains an optional EstimatedPromptTokens field; evaluateCandidate rejects (or penalizes) candidates whose concrete_model's known context window is below that. Uses v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits + catalog ContextWindow fallback.

2. **Tool-calling support gating** — needs per-provider / per-flavor capability metadata. Likely populated from discovery (/v1/models/status) + hand-curated capability table for harnesses.

3. **Effort / permissions gating** — mostly already in place for harnesses (Harness.EffortFlag checked at routing.go:rejectIfNotViable) but provider-specific gating (e.g., 'openrouter doesn't honor effort on non-reasoning models') is not.

4. **Structured-output support** — probably lowest priority; most reasoning-tool-capable models also do structured output.

This bead's AC is to file the sub-beads (smaller, focused) and close THIS bead once the sub-beads exist. The epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on those sub-beads for its exit gate — context-window etc. are a Phase 2 concern. The epic is about correct resolution; capability gating is about preventing resolved-but-unrunnable routes.

## Files touched (by sub-beads, not this one)

- cli/internal/agent/types.go — CandidatePlan capability fields, RouteRequest capability hints
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — capability checks in rejectIfNotViable + scoring
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go — ContextWindow, supports_tools, supports_structured_output per ModelEntry/Surface",

.ddx/executions/20260418T064927-78e29296/prompt.md:5:## Problem (filed per codex + opus fresh-eyes review 2026-04-17)

Both reviews flagged the same missing concept: routing currently scores candidates on cost class, quota, and historical success rate — but does NOT gate on whether the resolved route can actually execute the requested work. Concrete failure modes this lets through:

1. **Context-window overflow.** Bead prompt is 80k tokens. Fuzzy match picks a 32k-context model on the pinned provider. Routing 'succeeds'. Dispatch fails mid-stream with a provider 400 or truncated output.

2. **Missing tool-calling support.** Some omlx flavors or older local servers don't implement /v1/chat/completions with tools. Routing picks them via fuzzy match. Dispatch fails with 'tools not supported' or silently returns a no-tool response that the execute-bead loop can't use.

3. **Missing structured-output support.** Some provider/model combos don't implement JSON-mode / tool-use-required. If the bead prompt relies on it, routing-level blindness means the failure only surfaces at dispatch.

4. **Effort / permissions.** --effort high or --permissions unrestricted may not be honored by every harness+provider combination. Routing should reject or downgrade rather than dispatch and fail.

Codex: 'routing currently scores mostly on cost/quota/history, not whether a provider/model can satisfy tool calling, effort controls, permissions, or context-window needs.'
Opus: 'If the bead prompt is 80k tokens and the fuzzy-matched model is 32k, routing succeeds and dispatch fails mid-stream. Omlx flavor may not support tools at all.'

## Out of scope for this bead

This bead tracks the CONCEPT as a sibling of the main autorouting epic, but a full capability-constraint scoring system is a larger change than the current Phase-1 scope. Per opus: 'Scope this out of the epic explicitly (don't expand), but add a sibling bead so it's tracked.'

Split out into sub-scope bites is TBD:

1. **Context-window gating** — smallest useful slice. RouteRequest gains an optional EstimatedPromptTokens field; evaluateCandidate rejects (or penalizes) candidates whose concrete_model's known context window is below that. Uses v0.3.11's LookupModelLimits + catalog ContextWindow fallback.

2. **Tool-calling support gating** — needs per-provider / per-flavor capability metadata. Likely populated from discovery (/v1/models/status) + hand-curated capability table for harnesses.

3. **Effort / permissions gating** — mostly already in place for harnesses (Harness.EffortFlag checked at routing.go:rejectIfNotViable) but provider-specific gating (e.g., 'openrouter doesn't honor effort on non-reasoning models') is not.

4. **Structured-output support** — probably lowest priority; most reasoning-tool-capable models also do structured output.

This bead's AC is to file the sub-beads (smaller, focused) and close THIS bead once the sub-beads exist. The epic ddx-2d974641 does NOT depend on those sub-beads for its exit gate — context-window etc. are a Phase 2 concern. The epic is about correct resolution; capability gating is about preventing resolved-but-unrunnable routes.

## Files touched (by sub-beads, not this one)

- cli/internal/agent/types.go — CandidatePlan capability fields, RouteRequest capability hints
- cli/internal/agent/routing.go — capability checks in rejectIfNotViable + scoring
- cli/internal/agent/catalog.go — ContextWindow, supports_tools, supports_structured_output per ModelEntry/Surface

The bead's <description> and <acceptance> sections above are the completion contract — every AC checkbox must be provably satisfied by a specific piece of code, test, or file you can point to after your commit.

## How to work

1. Read first. If the bead description names files, read them to see what is already there. If the bead references a spec, ADR, or API contract, read the relevant sections before writing code. Do not start editing until you understand what the bead wants changed and why.

2. Cross-reference your work against the acceptance criteria as you go. Before committing, walk through the AC one item at a time and identify the specific test name, file path, or function that satisfies each checkbox. If you cannot point to concrete evidence for an AC item, it is not done.

3. Run the project's verification commands before committing. Use whatever commands match the crate or package you touched — typically `cargo test` and `cargo clippy` for Rust, `bun test` or `npm test` for JS/TS, `go test` for Go. The bead description usually names the exact commands. **Do not commit red code.** If a test or lint fails, fix it first.

4. Commit once, when everything is green. Stage only the files you intentionally changed with `git add <specific-paths>` — never `git add -A`, because there may be unrelated WIP in this worktree. Use a conventional-commit-style subject ending with `[<bead-id>]`. DDx will merge your commits back to the base branch.

5. If you cannot complete the bead in this pass, write your reasoning to `.ddx/executions/20260416T203015-be7ac4f6/no_changes_rationale.txt` with: (a) what is done, (b) what is blocking, (c) what a follow-up attempt would need. Do NOT commit partial, exploratory, or red code hoping the reviewer will accept it — a well-justified no_changes is better than a bad commit. The bead will be re-queued for another attempt, potentially with a stronger model.

## The bead contract overrides project defaults

The bead description and AC override any CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or project-level conservative defaults in this worktree. If the bead asks for new documentation, write it. If the bead adds a new module or crate, add it. Conservative rules (YAGNI, DOWITYTD, no-docs-unless-asked) do not apply inside execute-bead — the bead IS the ask.

## Quality bar and the review step

After your commit merges, an automated review step may check your work against the acceptance criteria. If any AC item is unmet, the bead reopens and escalates to a higher-capability model, and the review findings are threaded into the next attempt's prompt as a `<review-findings>` section. **The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet the AC in this pass so the bead closes cleanly.**

If this prompt already contains a `<review-findings>` section, address every BLOCKING finding before claiming the work complete. Those findings are the precise list of what the previous attempt missed.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — the workspace is already initialized. Running it corrupts the bead queue and project configuration.
- Do not modify files outside the scope the bead description names.
- Do not rewrite CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or any other project-instructions file unless the bead explicitly asks for it.

## When the work is done

After the commit succeeds and you have verified every AC item, stop. Return control to the orchestrator. Do not continue to explore the repository, run extra tests, or generate follow-up notes — the bead is complete, and returning promptly is how execute-bead signals success.
